g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we explore two powerful graph algorithms: Warshall's for reachability and Floyd's for shortest paths.
Transitive closure determines which vertices can reach other vertices in a directed graph through any path.
g.text: Finds if a path exists between
Edge (u,v) in G' if path u→v exists in G

g.data-narration: The all-pairs shortest path problem finds the minimum distance between every pair of vertices in a weighted graph.
g.text: D[i][j] = shortest distance from i to j

g.data-narration: Warshall's algorithm iteratively improves the reachability matrix using boolean OR and AND operations.
g.text: k = intermediate vertex (1 to n), i = source, j = destination

g.data-narration: First, initialize the reachability matrix with direct edges from the adjacency matrix - 1 for edge, 0 otherwise.
g.text: Initialize R[i][j] = 1 if edge i→j exists

g.data-narration: With k=1, we check if paths through vertex 1 provide new reachability. No new paths found here.
g.text: Check: R[i][j] = R[i][j] OR (R[i][1] AND R[1][j])

g.data-narration: With k=2, vertex 1 can reach vertex 3 via vertex 2, so we set R[1][3] = 1. This discovers a new path!
With k=3, we discover paths from vertices 1 and 2 to vertex 4 through the chain 1→3→4 and 2→3→4.
After k=4, the final transitive closure shows complete reachability: 1 reaches all, 2 reaches 3 and 4, 3 reaches 4.
g.text: Vertex 1 reaches all vertices via transitive paths!

g.data-narration: Floyd's algorithm finds shortest paths using minimum comparison: keep the shorter path between direct and via vertex k.
g.text: k = intermediate vertex, i = source, j = destination
through k, take the shorter one"

g.data-narration: Initialize the distance matrix with direct edge weights, infinity for non-edges, and zero on the diagonal.
With vertex 1 as intermediate, no new shorter paths are found since no vertex can reach vertex 1.
g.text: No path can reach vertex 1, so no improvements through k=1

g.data-narration: With vertex 2 as intermediate, we find shorter paths: 1→4 becomes 8 (via 2), and 3→4 becomes 5 (via 2).
With vertex 3 as intermediate, checking if paths through 3 improve distances. No further improvements in this iteration.
After k=4, the final distance matrix shows all shortest paths. For example, 1→4 = 8 via vertex 2, not directly.
Both algorithms share O(n³) complexity but differ: Warshall uses boolean operations for reachability, Floyd uses min for distances.
In summary: Warshall's finds if you can reach, Floyd's finds how far. Both are essential dynamic programming tools for graphs.
g.text: • Warshall's Algorithm: Computes transitive closure (reachability) using boolean matrix
• Floyd's Algorithm: Computes all-pairs shortest paths using distance matrix
• Both run in O(n³) time - efficient for dense graphs
• Key: k-loop must be outermost for correct dynamic programming approach

g.data-narration: Thank you for learning! Remember to practice tracing both algorithms on sample graphs for exam success.
g.text: university 2022 Scheme - CSE | Graph Algorithms - Unit 3
